'''Barry Eaton''' (born 30 September 1973) is a Welsh former international [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s, and [[Coach (sport)|coach]]ed in the 2000s and 2010s. He played at representative level for [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]], and at club level for [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]], [[Wakefield Trinity]], [[Dewsbury Rams]], [[Castleford Tigers]], [[Widnes Vikings]], [[Batley Bulldogs]] and the [[Keighley Cougars]], as a {{rlp|so|sh}} or {{rlp|hk}},<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> and coached at club level for [[Keighley Cougars]] and [[Hunslet R.L.F.C.|Hunslet Hawks]].<ref name="Coach Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

==Background==
Eaton was born [[Wakefield]], [[West Riding of Yorkshire]], England.<ref name="wg">{{cite web |title=Eaton Signs Up With The Vikings |url=https://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/news/5280409.eaton-signs-up-with-the-vikings/ |website=Warrington Guardian |publisher=Newsquest Media Group |access-date=9 February 2022 |date=3 October 2001}}</ref> He is the uncle of the rugby league footballer; [[Danny Ansell]].

==International honours==
Eaton won [[Cap (sport)|cap]]s for Wales while at Dewsbury in the 17-24 defeat by Ireland at [[Vetch Field]], [[Swansea]] on Friday 15 October 1999, the 16-36 defeat by Scotland at [[Firhill Stadium]], [[Glasgow]] on Friday 22 October 1999, the 40-8 victory over South Africa at [[Loftus Versfeld Stadium]], [[Pretoria]] on Thursday 19 October 2000, and the 33-42 defeat by England at the [[Racecourse Ground]], [[Wrexham]] on Sunday 29 July 2001.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/><ref>{{cite book|first1= Graham|last1= Williams|first2= Peter|last2= Lush|first3= David|last3= Farrar|title= The British Rugby League Records Book|publisher= London League|isbn= 978-1-903659-49-6|year= 2009|pages= 108–114}}</ref>

==Playing career==
As a schoolboy, Barry Eaton played [[rugby league]] for [[Stanley Rangers]], and both rugby league, and [[association football|football]] (soccer) for both [[Wakefield]] district, and [[Yorkshire]] county, this was followed by an apprenticeship with football club [[Barnsley F.C.|Barnsley]] from 1990 to 1992, in 1993 he joined [[rugby league]] club [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]], gaining promotion to the [[Rugby Football League Championship Second Division|First Division]] in that year, he was briefly at [[Wakefield Trinity]] from August 1995 to November 1995, after which he joined [[Dewsbury Rams|Dewsbury]], in 1997 he was the "Supporters' Player of the Year", in 1999 he was a [[Trans-Pennine Cup]] and [[Rugby Football League Championship Second Division|First Division]] title winner, and a First Division Grand Final runner-up, he set both of Dewsbury's "goals in a season" and "points in a season" records, and was "Supporters' Player of the Year" again, in 2000 he played and scored in every game, and was a [[Rugby Football League Championship Second Division|First Division]] title, and First Division Grand Final winner. In 2000 he had a loan spell with [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] in 2000's [[Super League V]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Eaton the cure for injury-hit Castleford |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/eaton-the-cure-for-injuryhit-castleford-695976.html |website=The Independent |access-date=9 February 2022 |date=5 August 2000}}</ref>

In 2001 he joined [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]],<ref name="wg" /> and played in all but three of their 28 matches in 2002's [[Super League VII]]. In October 2002 he joined [[Rugby Football League Championship Second Division|Northern Ford Premiership]] club [[Batley Bulldogs|Batley]], he played and scored in all 35-games first team games in season 2003, and in all 30-games in season 2004, he established a new world record by landing 38 consecutive successful goal kicks between 29 June 2003 and 24 August 2003, and set Batley's "goals in a season" record, he was "Supporters' Player of the Year", and "Coaches' Player of the Year", between 2002 and 2005 he played and scored in 70 consecutive games, and equalled record for fastest century of goals from start of season in 17-games.<ref name="Barry Eaton testimonial website">{{cite web|url=http://www.barry-testimonial.org|title=Barry Eaton testimonial website|publisher=barry-testimonial.org|date=31 December 2011|accessdate=1 January 2012}}{{dead link|date=November 2018|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref>

==Coaching career==
Barry Eaton moved to [[Keighley Cougars|Keighley]] as [[player-coach]] in 2005. He retired from playing and was given full control of the team when [[Peter Roe (rugby league)|Peter Roe]] became Director of Rugby in 2007,<ref>{{cite web |title=Coach content to move upstairs |url=https://www.thetelegraphandargus.co.uk/sport/rlkeighleycougars/1164533.coach-content-to-move-upstairs/ |website=Bradford Telegraph and Argus |access-date=21 January 2024 |date=1 February 2007}}</ref> although he continued to make sporadic on-field appearances during that season. In both 2008 and 2009 he was nominated for [[RFL League 1|Championship 1]] coach of the year.<ref>{{cite web |title=Eaton in the running for top award |url=https://www.keighleynews.co.uk/sport/3666810.eaton-in-the-running-for-top-award/ |website=Keighley News |access-date=21 January 2024 |date=11 September 2008}}</ref> After leading Keighley to promotion, he was unable to keep them in the [[Championship (rugby league)|Co-operative Championship]], he then moved to [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]] to become assistant to [[Iestyn Harris]] for 2011's [[Super League XVI]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Crusaders appoint Eaton |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/15612/6563173/crusaders-appoint-eaton |website=Sky Sports |access-date=21 January 2024 |date=9 December 2010}}</ref> He then moved to [[Hunslet Hawks]] in August 2011.<ref>{{cite web |title=Eaton takes over at Hunslet Hawks |url=https://www.thetelegraphandargus.co.uk/sport/rlkeighleycougars/9254670.eaton-takes-over-at-hunslet-hawks/ |website=Bradford Telegraph and Argus |access-date=21 January 2024 |date=15 September 2011}}</ref>
